test_id_1.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_1.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent and provides more detailed guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, particularly regarding Medicare enrollment requirements and payment limitations. Policy documents, with the newest relevant update from February 1, 2024, primarily refer providers to the manual for pharmacy claims guidance and reiterate that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare.", "policy_definition": "For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 1, 2023]. Dual eligible members will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021]. Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 1, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 1, 2025]. All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and this payment must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021] and [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — August 1, 2019]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members, including different types of Medicare enrollment (CMS-460, CMS-855B, CMS-855S) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 1, 2025], which is not detailed in the policy documents.", "The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider for dual eligible members will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025], a detail not present in the policy documents.", "Policy documents refer providers to external billing guidelines and manuals for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 1, 2023], whereas the provider manual itself contains specific requirements for dual eligible claims."], "caveats": null}

test_id_2.query: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_2.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update from February 1, 2024, and therefore should receive more weight for current guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims. The provider manual emphasizes mandatory Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, while policy documents primarily address immunization services for dual eligibles.", "policy_definition": "Pharmacies are only able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. This guidance was reiterated in August 2019, stating that dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — August 01, 2019]. For general pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]. Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources acknowledge the role of Medicare for dual eligible members. The policy documents state that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], and the provider manual mandates that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents primarily focus on immunization services for dual eligible members, stating they access these services through Medicare, and generally refer providers to other billing guidelines for pharmacy claims without detailing specific dual eligible payment rules [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]."], "caveats": null}

Medicaid managed care (MMC) enrollees access immunization services through their health plans, and providers should contact the individual plan for billing guidance [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 1, 2021]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program mandates Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from appropriately enrolled providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 1, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 1, 2025]. For general pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]. Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources acknowledge the role of Medicare for dual eligible members. The policy documents state that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], and the provider manual mandates that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. test_id_6.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims
test_id_6.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, emphasizing that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, reiterates Medicaid's role as the payer of last resort and the requirement to bill other liable third parties first.", "policy_definition": "New York State Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment, which is managed through coordination of benefits (COB) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. Pharmacies are responsible for actively seeking Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients and for timely billing to the appropriate party, including commercial plans or Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].", "provider_manual_definition": "For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would receive for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025]. Providers must determine if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other applicable third parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].", "Both sources highlight the pharmacy's responsibility to ensure proper billing to other insurance carriers before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that the combined payment must be accepted as payment in full, with no additional payment if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.", "The provider manual specifies that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. test_id_11.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_11.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid program updated its Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025, emphasizing that \"duals are subject to Medicare rules\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees are directed to \"continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid generally \"only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to these rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources imply that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly prohibits pharmacy providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.", "The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will only pay for certain drug classes excluded from Medicare Part D coverage for dual-eligible members, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.", "The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and no additional payment is made if Medicare's payment is greater than the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific payment cap not mentioned in the policy documents.", "Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-related co-payment responsibilities."], "caveats": null}

test_id_12.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_12.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, specifically stating that pharmacy providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, broadly states that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth, and Medicaid is the payer of last resort for general billing. Given the provider manual's later effective date and specific focus on pharmacy co-payments, it should receive more weight for current guidance on this topic.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2025, dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. For general billing, Medicaid is considered the payer of last resort, meaning other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees continue to access them through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Both Medicare and Medicaid payments (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for services for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary or last-resort payer [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources acknowledge that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment for pharmacy services will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and if Medicare pays more, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail this specific payment cap or acceptance as payment in full.", "Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and that duals are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Medicare Part D and Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_14.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_14.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid program updated its Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025, emphasizing that \"duals are subject to Medicare rules\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that \"Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted\" and that \"potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid\" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees are instructed to \"continue to access immunization services through Medicare\" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit \"cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that \"the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient\" and that \"if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, it is generally \"not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug\" unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as \"Select prescription vitamins\" and \"Certain non-prescription drugs\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to these rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], and that duals are subject to Medicare rules [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01].", "Both sources imply that Medicaid will only cover services or costs not covered by Medicare for dual-eligible members [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly prohibits pharmacy providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.", "The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will not make additional payments if the Medicare payment for a service is greater than the Medicaid fee, and that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment cannot exceed what a Medicaid-only patient would receive [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. This level of detail regarding payment caps is not present in the policy documents.", "The provider manual provides specific guidance on billing Medicaid for Medicare Part D members, stating it's generally not appropriate unless the drug class is specifically excluded from Part D plans (e.g., select vitamins, certain non-prescription drugs) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents do not offer this specific guidance for Part D.", "Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], which is not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts."], "caveats": null}

test_id_15.query: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?
test_id_15.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligibles are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, effective July 1, 2025, dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules regarding telehealth services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees continue to access them through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — 2019-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that dual-eligible members are primarily covered by Medicare for certain services, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.", "The provider manual provides specific guidance on when Medicaid will pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members (only for drug classes excluded by law from Part D plans) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.", "The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific financial constraint not present in the policy excerpts.", "Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — 2019-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Part B and Part D."], "caveats": null}

test_id_18.query: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?
test_id_18.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (May 1, 2023) and provides more specific guidance on Medicaid's role in covering Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. Policy documents, while older, generally indicate that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, but also mention items not subject to the carve-out that should continue to be billed to the member's Medicare plan.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2023, New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy, with practitioner administered drugs (PADs) billed directly to NYRx listed on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document from July 1, 2020, states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Another policy document from March 1, 2021, notes that certain items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document are not subject to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s Medicare plan [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Medicaid only pays for these excluded drugs if they are on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. For dual-eligible members, all claims must first be submitted to Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply that Medicaid's coverage for drugs is secondary to Medicare, requiring claims to be submitted to Medicare first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources refer to a list of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs for coverage [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, providing examples like select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents, while mentioning items not subject to carve-out that should be billed to Medicare, do not provide this specific legal exclusion criteria for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].", "The provider manual details payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this level of detail regarding payment caps for Part D-excluded drugs.", "An older policy document states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], which contrasts with the provider manual's explicit allowance for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs that are on the reimbursable list."], "caveats": null}

test_id_30.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization from a third party does not negate the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid. The newest policy document, effective November 1, 2021, also emphasizes that all available resources must be used before Medicaid payment, and prior authorization issues must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. Given the provider manual's more recent effective date, it should receive more weight for current guidance.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update effective November 1, 2021, federal regulations mandate that all available resources be utilized before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, meaning a responsible third party must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01]. An earlier policy update from July 1, 2020, further clarifies that any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines effective October 1, 2025, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate that a provider pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid, and if there is a responsible third party, they must pay first. Crucially, the manual states that \"Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility\" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also notes that prior authorization and editing on prescriptions for all topical compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — 2025-10-01]. For billing purposes, if a prescription requires prior approval or authorization, the number must be entered in Field 12 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].", "similarities": ["Both sources emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources indicate that if a responsible third party exists, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that \"Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility\" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a direct statement not found in the provided policy documents.", "The policy documents explicitly state that \"Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid\" and that \"Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid\" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. While the provider manual implies this through the responsibility to pursue other coverage, it does not contain this exact phrasing regarding resolution of PA issues with the third party or the direct consequence of denial for failure to submit to the third party.", "The provider manual details specific requirements for prior authorization on topical compounded drug products for NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the provided policy excerpts."], "caveats": null}

test_id_31.query: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?
test_id_31.answer: {"headline_summary": "The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on Coordination of Benefits (COB) requirements. Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill applicable third parties first.", "policy_definition": "New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. This process is managed through Coordination of Benefits (COB) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. COB ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "For Workers’ Compensation cases, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if the drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer by using an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual imply that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other liable parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy documents provide a general requirement for COB, stating that providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. In contrast, the provider manual specifically details COB requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases, outlining when Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as the primary payer for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": "The provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy-related COB requirements, particularly concerning Workers' Compensation, while the policy documents provide a broader, more general definition of COB for all Medicaid providers. The provider manual does not explicitly state the federal regulation requirement mentioned in the policy documents."}

test_id_36.query: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing
test_id_36.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, and should receive more weight by default. It states that pharmacists are not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available or the prescription is legitimate, and they must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and prescriber's identity. The newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, focus on contacting the PCP for emergency prescriptions and consulting the prescriber for prescription directions.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, effective January 1, 2025, if prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment in filling them without regard to restriction requirements and for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Additionally, providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source, effective October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": [], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly addresses missing patient information such as address, age, or CIN, stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not directly address these specific missing patient data points.", "The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents mention consulting the prescriber for directions [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01] and notifying the prescriber for prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], but not the initial verification of an unknown prescriber's identity.", "Policy documents outline responsibilities for emergency prescriptions, including using professional judgment and contacting the PCP the next business day if the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], which is not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts."], "caveats": null}

test_id_40.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, and should receive more weight by default. It clarifies that pharmacists are not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, and emphasizes verifying prescriber identity. The newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, focus on contacting PCPs for emergency prescriptions and consulting prescribers for prescription directions.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, effective January 1, 2025, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment in filling emergency prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the assigned PCP is unavailable, and must contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Additionally, providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information if it is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions, either by verifying information or consulting with prescribers [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient information (address, age, CIN) if it's readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents focus on contacting prescribers for clarification on directions or prior authorization for non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].", "Policy documents outline responsibilities related to emergency prescriptions, requiring pharmacists to use professional judgment and contact PCPs the next business day if unavailable, a detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]."], "caveats": null}

test_id_41.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. This exclusion is also noted in the April 2022 Medicaid Update, which specifies \"agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)]\" are excluded for both FFS and MMC programs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The January 2025 Medicaid Update lists \"drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid\" as examples of drugs excluded from State Plan Coverage [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under the New York State Medicaid Program. The Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, states that \"Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Socia [...]\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. It further clarifies that \"Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, [...]\" are examples of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for exclusion of certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy documents, specifically the April 2022 Medicaid Update, also mention \"agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain\" as excluded, and cite NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) in addition to federal regulations [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. test_id_43.answer: {"headline_summary": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default.", "policy_definition": "NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. The New York State Medicaid Update from April 2024 states that \"Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)... including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.\" [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. This exclusion is also noted in the April 2022 Medicaid Update, which specifies \"agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain\" are excluded for both FFS and MMC programs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The January 2025 Medicaid Update also lists \"drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid\" as examples of drugs excluded from State Plan Coverage [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "Drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under the New York State Medicaid Program. The Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly states that \"Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)\" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. It further clarifies that \"Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain\" are examples of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources mention that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications like weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].", "The policy documents specifically mention the exclusion for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], while the provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly differentiate between these program areas for this exclusion."], "caveats": null}

test_id_46.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides a more detailed and current explanation of 340B drug reimbursement, stating it is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost, while the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2022, focuses on identification requirements for 340B drugs. The provider manual should receive more weight as it is significantly more recent.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source, the \"New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8\" effective July 1, 2022, 340B drugs must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01]. Earlier policy documents, such as the \"New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9\" effective August 1, 2019, state that 340B drug claims are required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care (MMC) members and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01]. If the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, the claim will be denied [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - 2019-08-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual, the \"New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines\" effective October 1, 2025, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01]. When submitting 340B drug claims in the NCPDP format, specific fields are required, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, and the Ingredient Cost Submitted as the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].
